title: Worker Safety and Health Requirements to Support Reform of Nuclear Reactor Testing

comments_clusters.representative_excerpt: Despite making changes with Department-wide implications, DOE did not conduct a programmatic analysis assessing how the rule affects other DOE missions, sites, or workforces.

The proposal fails to evaluate or monetize the potential costs associated with increased injuries, occupational illnesses, safety incidents, or work stoppages that may result from reduced safety requirements. Ignoring foreseeable safety-related costs undermines the credibility and completeness of the Department’s regulatory analysis.

DOE fails to justify cost savings with rigorous analysis. The proposal includes no transparent methodology, assumptions, or detailed accounting of savings. Moreover, DOE does not analyze long-term financial costs associated with increased injuries, illness, corrective actions, or potential project delays. This renders the economic justification unreliable. I...
The proposed rule does not address how less structured safety programs will affect consistency and quality of occurrence reporting, trending, and root cause analysis across sites.
DOE provides no analysis of how removing clear safety expectations will affect safety culture, including willingness to pause/stop work, raise concerns, or challenge unsafe conditions.
The rule represents a shift away from DOE’s longstanding emphasis on structured, preventive worker protection without adequate justification.
The proposal alters or relies on revised definitions central to Part 851 without evaluating impacts on contracts, enforcement actions, compliance determinations, or legal accountability.

DOE fails to demonstrate how the proposed changes maintain protection levels required under the Atomic Energy Act and other relevant statutes. Absent statutory risk assessment, the rule appears to unlawfully prioritize administrative ease over legal obligations for worker safety.

The proposed § 851.46 would exempt ONR facilities from: ∙ DOE approval of safety and health programs ∙ Mandatory occupational medicine programs ∙ Mandatory radiation protection program alignment ∙ Mandatory recordkeeping requirements ∙ Appendix A functional area requirements ∙ Incorporation by reference of consensus safety standards (ACGIH, ANSI) ∙ Independe...

Cost Savings Cannot Justify Statutory Violation DOE’s preamble emphasizes $20-60 million in annual cost savings. However: a) Statute Does Not Authorize Cost-Benefit Balancing: 42 U.S.C. § 2282c requires substantially equivalent protection “to the extent practicable.” It does not authorize DOE to reduce protection to save money. Congress made the policy decis...

Under section 4b of EO 14301, Reforming Nuclear Reactor Testing at the Department of Energy (DOE), the DOE seeks to revise regulations, guidance, procedures, and practice...to significantly expedite the review, approval, and deployment of advanced reactors under the Department's jurisdiction (Trump, 2025). Whenever rapid deployments of new technologies are c...

Regarding the exclusion of American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH) Threshold Limit Values (TLVs) for Chemical Substances and Physical Agents and Biological Exposure Indices (BEIs) (2016): The ACGIH publishes annual updates based on current toxicological information to establish TLVs. Permissible Exposure Limits (PELs) identified in ...

The proposed rule is arbitrary and capricious under 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A) because it: 1. Relies on conclusory assertions without supporting data 2. Fails to consider important aspects of the problem 3. Offers explanations that run counter to the evidence 4. Creates irrational distinctions between similarly-situated workers Specific Arbitrariness Issues: 1. Un...

Assertions that the rule reduces burden are not supported by empirical data, contractor surveys, or historical cost comparisons. DOE was mandated to create 10 CFR 851 by congress due to its failure on holding contractors accountable to prevent injuries. DOE must provide this data.

10 CFR 851.10(b)(1) only requires a DOE contractor's WSH Program to comply with “Requirements set forth in subpart C of this part that are applicable to the hazards associated with the contractor's scope of work"

The NPRM states “Additionally, some sections include standard or codes that have been revised or updated, which contractors are not able to utilize because specific revision numbers are referenced” This is not accurate. 10 CFR 851.12(b) and .23(b) allow DOE contractors to take additional actions and comply with any additional safety and health requirements t...

Cherry-Picked Example (Cold Stress) The preamble’s sole concrete example is that TLV cold stress requirements are unnecessary at Idaho National Laboratory because “workers are acclimated” to sub-61degrees F temperatures. This misrepresents the science, Cold stress TLVs aren’t based on worker comfort but on preventing cold-related injuries (frostbite, hypothe...

ANSI Standards What’s eliminated: Mandatory compliance with: ∙ ANSI Z88.2 (Respiratory Protection) ∙ ANSI Z49.1 (Welding Safety) Why respiratory protection elimination is especially problematic: ∙ Nuclear facilities involve airborne radioactive contamination ∙ Respiratory protection is last line of defense against inhalation hazards ∙ ANSI Z88.2 ensures prop...

The proposed rulemaking acknowledges that DOE’s worker safety and health program must continue to protect workers while incorporating decades of operating experience and supporting responsible innovation—an intent I strongly support. While expediting the review and approval of advanced reactors may be necessary to support emerging technologies, it is essenti...

comments_clusters.representative_excerpt: As a Masters-prepared Registered Nurse in Pediatrics, I must speak up on behalf of women and children, as they are the persons most affected negatively by radiation. Women have twice the cancer rate as men and children have 5 times the cancer rate as adults. A recent study in Massachusetts published by Harvard found cancer rates are higher near nuclear power...

comments_clusters.representative_excerpt: Insufficient Public Comment Period The 30-day comment period is inadequate for proposed rule of this scope and complexity. Why More Time Is Needed: 1. Complexity of Changes ∙ Rule affects multiple sections of Part 851 ∙ Requires analysis of interactions with Part 835, Part 830, and other DOE regulations ∙ Necessitates review of incorporated standards (ACGIH,...

National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) Violation DOE’s categorical exclusion determination is improper. National Environmental Policy Act requires environmental review of major federal actions significantly affecting human environment. “Human environment” includes worker health and safety. This Rule Has Significant Effects: ∙ Reduces safety protections for...

DOE fails to evaluate whether weaker safety requirements could increase contractor insurance premiums or liability exposure. Which is why 10 CFR 851 was created with indemnification by congress due to the unique nuclear hazards, by changing this, it would increase the burden on to the tax payers.

Downgrading consensus standards from mandatory to optional weakens reliance on best available safety practices. Congress mandated to keep the same level of Safety which included these consensus standards when 10 CFR 851 was created.

As an DOE contractor employed Occupational Health and Safety Professional who specializes in Industrial Hygiene (CIH) and Workplace Safety (CSP) I would like to weigh in on the removal of requirements and reference to the 2016 ACGIH TLV's & BEI's for favor of solely using OSHA regulations. OSHA's occupational exposure limits (OELs(PELs)) are mostly legacy li...

Regarding "The reduction in compliance-related activities would likely increase cost savings, allowing contractors to reallocate resources previously devoted to paperwork and approvals toward strengthening safety programs, training initiatives, and other critical areas” What cost savings? How much? Where is the economic analysis? 851 has been in place for 20...

851.46 states 851.24 does not apply to DOE sites under DOE's Office of Nuclear Energy responsibility. This would mean that these sites don’t have to comply with Appendix A.4 which states that contractors must ensure that all pressure vessels, boilers, air receivers, and supporting piping systems conform to the applicable ASME BPVC and B31codes for pressure p...

The proposed § 851.46 directly contravenes the explicit statutory requirement that DOE regulations “provide a level of protection for workers at such facilities that is substantially equivalent to the level of protection currently provided to such workers at such facilities.” Mandatory Nature of Statute: Congress used mandatory language (“shall promulgate”) ...

Illogical Cost-Benefit Analysis DOE estimates cost savings of “$20-60 million per year” for INL from: ∙ Reduced expenditures on ventilation and PPE ∙ Removing “overly-conservative work restrictions”. Savings from removing safety equipment is not a benefit: Eliminating hardhats would also save money but increase injuries. Cost reduction from removing protecti...

§ 851.46(b)(3) & (4): Elimination of Functional Area Requirements and Incorporated Standards Exempts ONR facilities from: ∙ § 851.24 (structured approach to functional areas) ∙ § 851.27(b), (c)(1), (c)(2) (incorporation by reference of ACGIH, ANSI standards). Appendix A Functional Areas. What’s eliminated: Mandatory compliance with Appendix A. These are foun...

Inadequate Analysis Under Executive Order 12866 Challenge: DOE’s regulatory impact analysis fails to meet EO 12866 requirements for significant regulatory actions. Required Elements Missing: No Baseline Data ∙ No current injury/illness rates at affected facilities ∙ No analysis of incidents prevented by current requirements ∙ No worker exposure data ∙ No com...

The proposed rule creates incentives that undermine safety. 1. Race to the Bottom If Office of Nuclear Energy facilities gain competitive advantage through reduced safety compliance costs, other DOE offices may seek similar exemptions. Result: Pressure to weaken safety across DOE complex. 2. Contractor Selection Bias Contractors willing to accept lowest safe...

Proposed Change Results in -DOE must approve safety programs before implementation (§ 851.11), now No DOE approval required, and Workers exposed to hazards under unapproved programs. -Mandatory occupational medicine program (§ 851.23(a)(9)) and Eliminated required medical surveillance, baseline exams, or occupational physician input -Nuclear facilities could...

I object to the DOE’s proposed amendments to the regulations for worker safety and health to expedite the review, approval, and deployment of advanced reactors under DOE's jurisdiction. I am concerned that these revisions may compromise the continued protection of workers. As with the exclusions for the reactors, these amendments were written without input f...

DOE asserts that the rule reduces burden and increases efficiency but provides no quantified cost-benefit analysis as required under Executive Order 12866 and OMB Circular A-4. Conclusory statements about efficiency do not substitute for a reasoned economic analysis, particularly where worker injury and illness risks are affected.

Re-definition narrows statutory protection without justification. Limiting the term to “employees of a DOE contractor” potentially excludes subcontractors, temporary workers, and other individuals who may nonetheless face the same hazards but would lose coverage under 10 Part 851. DOE provides no analysis of the risk distribution or rationale for excluding c...

I support the Department of Energy’s proposed rule to revise worker safety and health requirements for nuclear reactor testing reform. Updating these regulations is important to align safety programs with modern reactor design and construction activity, and to clarify employer responsibilities for hazard analysis and worker protection. In particular, the add...

In regards to "contractors subject to the new § 851.46 may choose to use appendix A (Worker Safety and Health Functional Areas) as guidance, but are not required to comply with appendix A. This is because, once the proposed revisions take effect, appendix A will be mandatory only for those contractors that are not operating under the responsibility of the Of...

Regarding "For example, TLVs for cold stress require that special protection of the hands be used if fine work is to be performed with bare hands for more than 10 to 20 minutes in an environment below 60.8 degrees Fahrenheit. Special protection includes warm air jets, radiant heaters, or contact warm plates. However, the temperatures requiring controls noted...

Regarding "Threshold Limit Values (TLVs) referenced previously lead contractors to take excessive precautions or require personal protective equipment (PPE) when doing so would not be required for industry." The hierarchy of controls and use of PPE is most definitely required by OSHA. 1910.132 requires a written PPE hazard assessment. 1910.132(d)(1)(i): If s...
851.3 Definitions The definition for Closure facility and CSO are the same as in the current 851

Page 10 Removing "overly-prescriptive individualized approval requirements. How would DOE provide oversight to the contractor if the contractor is not required to submit a safety and health plan? Modify the approval process but do not eliminate it. Page 10 Excluding ACGIH TLVs OSHA does not regulate everything which is harmful to humans. ACGIH TLVs provide a...

The proposed rule creates two classes of DOE workers with fundamentally different safety protections based solely on which DOE office oversees their work, with no rational basis for the distinction. Thus creating a Arbitrary Classification Under § 851.46, workers at Office of Nuclear Energy facilities receive: ∙ No DOE approval of safety programs ∙ Appendix ...

§ 851.46(b)(1) Nuclear Regulatory Commission requires licensees to submit safety programs for NRC review and approval. DOE proposes the opposite for its nuclear facilities—less oversight for facilities it directly controls than NRC requires for commercial facilities.
§ 851.46(b)(1) No Enforcement Mechanism Without required DOE approval, how will DOE ensure programs meet Part 851 requirements before workers are exposed to hazards? The rule provides no answer.

§ 851.46(b)(1) Violates “Substantially Equivalent” Standard Current workers have protection of DOE review before programs take effect. Proposed workers have no such protection. This is objectively less protection, not “substantially equivalent.”Retain DOE approval requirements. If DOE believes approval processes are too slow, reform the approval process, don...

§ 851.46(c)(2): Delegated Variance Authority Regulatory Text: Variances may be approved by “cognizant Head of DOE Field Element or DOE employee with authority to approve the relevant safety basis.” Circumvents Regulatory Process Current system (Subpart D): ∙ Variance petitions reviewed by EHSS Director ∙ Decision made by Under Secretary ∙ Public docket with ...

§ 851.46(c)(3): Enforcement Consultation Requirement Regulatory Text: “Any enforcement action taken under this part must be performed after consultation with the cognizant Head of DOE Field Element…” This undermines Enforcement Independence, the problem is Requiring enforcement officials to “consult” with the facility’s oversight manager before taking enforc...

Inconsistent with Civil Penalty Authority 42 U.S.C. § 2282c(b) authorizes DOE to impose civil penalties for Part 851 violations. Courts have held that penalty authority must be exercised independently. Required “consultation” with facility managers appears to compromise this independence.

DOE claims it “considered but rejected” applying changes DOE-wide. This one-sentence dismissal does not constitute alternatives analysis. DOE should have analyzed: ∙ Targeted reforms to specific burdensome requirements ∙ Process improvements without eliminating substantive protections ∙ Pilot programs with data collection before full implementation ∙ Risk-ba...

The preamble lists alleged benefits (“flexibility,” “agility,” “innovation”) but provides: ∙ No metrics for measuring these benefits ∙ No quantification of impacts ∙ No data supporting claimed improvements there is No Cost-Benefit Comparison The $20-60M savings estimate: ∙ Lacks supporting documentation ∙ Counts only compliance cost reduction, not safety ben...

1. Conservative Decision-Making When safety is uncertain, err on the side of caution. DOE’s approach: When standards seem burdensome, eliminate them without demonstrating they’re unnecessary. 2. safety requires multiple independent safety layers. Current Part 851 provides: ∙ Prescriptive standards (Appendix A, incorporated standards) ∙ DOE oversight (program...

Undefined Terms 1. “Operations authorized by DOE” (revised DOE site definition) ∙ What constitutes “authorization”? ∙ Does contract = authorization? ∙ What about permits, agreements, licenses? ∙ Who decides if operation is “authorized”? Vague term creates jurisdictional uncertainty. 2. “Cognizant Head of DOE Field Element” ∙ Is this a position title or funct...

Section 851.46(b) vs. (c)(1) ∙ (b) says certain provisions “do not apply” ∙ (c)(1) says Appendix A is “applicable only as guidance” If Appendix A doesn’t apply, why mention it’s “applicable as guidance”? These provisions contradict.

Section 851.46(c)(2) variance authority If contractors are exempt from Subpart D variance process (§ 851.46(b)(5)), why do they need alternative variance process (§ 851.46(c)(2))? What are they seeking variances from if requirements don’t apply?

Withdraw proposed rule and engage in comprehensive stakeholder process to identify genuine regulatory improvement opportunities 2. Address specific issues: ∙ Retain DOE approval of safety programs (§ 851.11) ∙ Retain tlv requirement (§ 851.23(a)(10)) ∙ Retain Appendix A as mandatory (or provide equivalent replacement) ∙ Retain independent variance authority ...

This proposed rule is legally deficient, procedurally flawed, and substantively dangerous. The rule appears driven by desire to expedite reactor projects rather than genuine safety concerns. The appropriate response is to withdraw the proposed rule and develop evidence-based improvements through proper stakeholder engagement, regulatory analysis, and demonst...

DOE Provides No Evidence of Equivalence To comply with 42 U.S.C. § 2282c, DOE must demonstrate that the proposed changes maintain substantially equivalent protection. DOE has provided: ∙ No data showing current injury/illness rates or that current requirements are unnecessary ∙ No analysis of how eliminated protections will be replaced ∙ No evidence that “fl...
DOE fails to quantify either the claimed cost savings or the potential safety-related costs, contrary to EO 12866 and OMB Circular A-4 requirements.

the proposal lacks adequate economic analysis, fails to assess Department-wide impacts, introduces regulatory ambiguity, and weakens worker safety protections in high-hazard environments. DOE has not met the standard of reasoned decision-making required for rulemaking and should withdraw the proposal for comprehensive reevaluation.

DOE asserts cost savings and efficiency gains without quantifying benefits, costs, or risk tradeoffs, contrary to EO 12866 and OMB Circular A-4. The absence of monetized safety impacts, injury costs, or long-term liabilities renders the economic justification incomplete and unreliable.

By making previously mandatory safety requirements optional for one mission area, DOE establishes a precedent that other program offices may seek to replicate. DOE provides no analysis of how it will prevent regulatory erosion across the Department once this precedent is established.

The proposal modifies and introduces definitions that are used broadly within DOE’s worker safety and health framework, without assessing how these changes interact with existing departmental policies, directives, and contracts. Redefining or narrowing key terms without a Department-wide impact analysis risks inconsistent interpretation and uneven applicatio...

Historically, DOE has treated substantive changes to worker safety and health requirements as warranting robust economic and policy analysis. The Department offers no explanation for abandoning that approach here.

Executive Order 12866 requires agencies to demonstrate that regulatory alternatives were considered and that the chosen approach maximizes net benefits. DOE does neither. DOE’s failure to analyze reasonable alternatives violates the core decision-making principles of EO 12866.

The Department improperly treats this rulemaking as non-significant for purposes of Executive Order 12866, despite proposing substantive changes to worker safety requirements in high-hazard nuclear operations. Weakening mandatory safety standards in nuclear reactor testing constitutes a major policy shift with direct implications for worker health and safety...

DOE has not demonstrated that this proposal will reduce costs or improve efficiency. Instead, it appears to substitute short-term regulatory convenience for long-term operational risk, legal exposure, and worker harm. Without quantified savings, comprehensive cost analysis, or evidence that reduced requirements will not increase incidents or delays, DOE has ...
Throughout the proposed rule, DOE relies on conclusory statements about efficiency and burden without citing empirical data, pilot results, or retrospective analyses.
DOE’s economic analysis excludes the costs of occupational illness and injury, including medical care, lost productivity, disability claims, and long-term health monitoring.

DOE does not acknowledge extensive evidence that robust safety programs reduce total project cost by preventing injuries, rework, equipment damage, and regulatory delays. The proposed rule selectively frames safety requirements as cost drivers rather than cost controls.

The proposed rule appears to shift costs rather than eliminate them, moving expenses from preventive safety programs to injury response, workers’ compensation, medical surveillance, and incident investigations. DOE has historically paid massive downstream costs for occupational disease claims. DOE provides no analysis of whether purported short-term savings ...

DOE asserts that removing mandatory consensus standards and structured safety requirements will reduce costs, yet provides no quantitative estimate of actual savings, no baseline comparison, and no contractor-specific cost data. Absent quantified savings, DOE cannot demonstrate that the benefits of deregulation outweigh the potential costs of increased injur...
DOE fails to provide adequate evidence that removed standards truly preserve worker health in high-hazard operations.

Congress required DOE to establish and enforce worker safety and health regulations because general industry standards and contractor discretion had failed to protect workers in DOE’s uniquely hazardous environments. The proposed rule reverses that statutory logic by weakening mandatory requirements, elevating efficiency over prevention, and expanding discre...

Congress expected DOE to rely on recognized industry consensus standards to establish a protective baseline; removing mandatory incorporation of these standards weakens protections without statutory justification. DOE offers no evidence that Congress intended DOE-specific safety rules to be less protective than established industry best practices.

Changes to definitions narrow the practical scope of worker safety obligations without congressional authorization and without analysis of downstream impacts on enforcement and compliance. Congress intended DOE safety requirements to be broadly protective; narrowing definitions through rulemaking undermines that intent.

DOE cannot use an Executive Order to override or undercut protections required by statute (e.g., 42 U.S.C. § 2282c) or to justify reducing safety standards that implement congressional intent to protect workers from occupational hazards. DOE’s assertion that safety will remain “substantially equivalent” is conclusory and not supported by evidence in the prop...

The proposed rule frames safety requirements primarily as administrative “burdens,” but worker safety is not merely a compliance cost; it is a statutory and ethical obligation. Characterizing protective standards as intrusive burdens prioritizes operational convenience over documented occupational risk reduction and contradicts DOE’s statutory mandate to red...

Creating a new regulatory carve-out for one office undermines uniform worker protection across the Department. Rather than improving safety, this exception introduces unequal regulatory treatment and weakens established protections for workers engaged in high-hazard construction and operation of nuclear facilities. DOE does not provide evidence that these ch...

(§ 851.11): Removing individualized approval undermines oversight and accountability. The individualized approval process currently ensures that contractor safety programs are tailored to specific hazards and comply with 10 Part 851 minimums. Eliminating this review weakens DOE’s ability to verify that contractor programs are effective, significantly increas...

Structured programs ensure systematic hazard assessment, control selection, and verification of effectiveness. Removing these obligations is not supported by evidence that alternative unstructured approaches maintain safety, and DOE fails to justify how unstructured methods meet statutory goals of the current rule.

Calling protective thresholds “overly conservative” misrepresents occupational health science. TLVs and similar measures are designed to protect against chronic and acute exposures, many of which are not addressed by OSHA minima. DOE provides only anecdotal examples (e.g., cold stress) but no comprehensive risk analysis. This rationale undermines scientific ...

DOE asserts that increased flexibility and reduced administrative tasks will improve worker engagement, safety, and operational performance. The claimed benefits are speculative and unsupported by measurable safety outcomes. Flexibility does not inherently improve safety; in high-hazard environments, increased discretion often correlates with greater variabi...

The proposes rule does not cite any analysis of historical injury/incident data that would support eliminating structural standards. Without such evidence, the claimed superior safety of less prescriptive approaches is unsubstantiated.

DOE certifies the proposed rule will not have significant economic impact on small entities but provides no supporting data. Considering expanded applicability and structural changes, this conclusion lacks analytical rigor.

The claimed benefits are speculative and unsupported by measurable safety outcomes. Flexibility does not inherently improve safety; in high-hazard environments, increased discretion often correlates with greater variability in hazard control and higher risk of oversight gaps. DOE offers no empirical evidence that reduced constraints better protect workers or...

DOE’s safety governance model depends on clear federal expectations coupled with contractor implementation. This rule reverses that balance by shifting too much authority to contractors without compensatory oversight mechanisms.
Reduced specificity in safety requirements will impair DOE’s ability to reconstruct compliance status during accident investigations, weakening causal analysis and lessons learned development.
Without baseline safety performance data, DOE cannot credibly evaluate whether the proposed changes improve or degrade worker protection.

Under an OSHA-only model, DOE lacked clear authority and tools to proactively oversee contractor safety programs, resulting in fragmented oversight and inconsistent safety performance across sites. The ability to hold the contractor accountable.

contractors were incentivized to meet minimum compliance thresholds rather than invest in robust safety systems, a dynamic DOE explicitly sought to correct through DOE-specific requirements. ⸻ DOE’s institutional history demonstrates that reliance on OSHA standards alone failed to prevent serious worker harm and systemic safety deficiencies, calling into que...

DOE’s own experience demonstrates that reliance on OSHA standards alone was insufficient to protect workers in DOE facilities, particularly in complex, high-hazard, and experimental environments. The Department adopted DOE-specific worker safety requirements precisely because OSHA-based compliance failed to prevent systemic hazards and long-term occupational...

The International Safety Equipment Association (ISEA) requests an extension of the Worker Safety Proposed Rule. The rule would make significant changes to current worker safety regulations, which require additional research.

Regarding "The benefits of these changes include increased flexibility, streamlined processes, cost savings, enhanced agility, and improved worker engagement.” How is worker engagement improved?
How do the changes for the variance process “enhance operational efficiency and safety for DOE contractors”?

No Rational Relationship to Safety DOE provides no explanation why the Nuclear reactor workers need less protection than fusion research workers, Nuclear fuel cycle workers need less protection than waste management workers, and Workers performing identical tasks (welding, construction, radiological work) deserve different protections based on funding source...

When DOE relied primarily on OSHA standards, chronic exposures,such as low-level radiation, toxic metals, and chemical byproducts,were inadequately addressed, resulting in long-term occupational illness that was not captured by acute injury metrics.

In regards to "Part 851 aim to streamline the regulatory framework for DOE's Office of Nuclear Energy contractors which will, in turn, enhance worker health and safety." How do these changes “enhance” worker safety and health? How does removing requirements “enhance” worker safety and health

In regard to “These changes align with the goals of efficiency and cost effectiveness, leading to better outcomes for DOE contractors and their workers” What is the better outcome for the workers? Less requirements to protect workers will not have a better outcome.
I will keep this fast and simple. Safety guidelines exist for a reason and to ignore them for the sake of speed is foolish.
Why include a new section, proposed § 851.46, to provide non-binding guidance and not as mandatory requirements. This is a regulation and everything should be mandatory

Regarding "The proposed revision to the definition of "DOE site" would currently apply only to one site, Idaho National Laboratory.” Is the intent to have multiple definitions for the same term in the same regulation? If so, that is confusing. If your intent was to say that this change would only impact INL that is untrue. What about Legacy Management sites?...

The NPRM states “Operational experience has demonstrated that appendix A creates confusion by, for example, requiring contractors to develop sections of a worker safety and health program that are not applicable to their scope of their work.” The current language clearly states (851.24) “contractors must comply with the applicable standards and provisions in...

Regarding "Part 851 aim to streamline the regulatory framework for DOE's Office of Nuclear Energy contractors which will, in turn, enhance worker health and safety." How do these changes “enhance” worker safety and health? How does removing requirements “enhance” worker safety and health
Regarding "These changes align with the goals of efficiency and cost effectiveness, leading to better outcomes for DOE contractors and their workers.” What is the better outcome for the workers?

Is it ethical to allow worker to be exposed to high levels of chemical or physical hazards to be different from DOE site to DOE site or from contractor to contractor on the same site based on the reactor mission. It is exposing workers to high levels of risk and with no supporting information in this NOPR to show that that risk should be taken and excused fo...

Regarding "Increased Flexibility: The revision of certain regulatory requirements would provide contractors with the ability to customize their safety and health programs to better align with their specific operational contexts. This flexibility allows contractors to implement tailored programs that can lead to the implementation of more effective and releva...
What is the benefit to the worker and their safety and health to eliminate DOE’s review and approval of the contractor’s worker safety and health program?
It seems extremely unethical to allow workers under NE to be exposed to contaminants at higher levels (e.g. PELs) compared to another DOE worker at another site subject to the TLVs.

This proposed rule is gives a false equivalence with OSHA Standards. DOE claims current standards (e.g., ACGIH TLVs) are “overly conservative” compared to OSHA requirements. ∙ ACGIH TLVs are industry best practice: Thousands of employers voluntarily adopt TLVs because they better protect workers than minimum OSHA requirements ∙ OSHA standards are floors, not...

The problem is that it removes independent oversight. The current system: DOE reviews and approves contractor safety programs before implementation, the proposed rule would allow Contractors self-certify their own safety programs. This creates obvious conflict of interest. Contractors have financial incentives to minimize safety costs. Independent DOE review...

Conflict of interest: Field Element Heads and facility managers have programmatic responsibilities to meet project schedules and budgets. They face inherent pressure to grant variances that expedite work even if safety margins are reduced. The current Under Secretary review ensures decisions are made by officials without direct project responsibilities. 3. N...

Accountability Gap If a variance leads to a serious incident, who is responsible? The rule creates ambiguity: ∙ Did Field Element Head properly approve? ∙ Did contractor properly implement variance? ∙ Did headquarters provide adequate oversight? Clear accountability is essential for safety. This provision undermines it.

Violates Separation of Duties Best practices in enforcement require separation between: ∙ Operational oversight (Field Element) ∙ Compliance monitoring (EHSS) ∙ Enforcement (separate enforcement office) This provision collapses these roles, allowing the same officials who oversee operations to influence enforcement against those operations.

Proposed rule ignores lessons from major nuclear events. Three Mile Island (1979): Key lesson: Regulatory complacency contributed to accident. NRC’s limited oversight allowed safety culture degradation. DOE response: Reduce oversight of DOE nuclear facilities. Give facility managers authority to grant variances from safety requirements. Assumption that exist...

Enforcement consultation requirement If fewer requirements apply to ONR facilities, what is there to enforce? And why does enforcement require consultation but initial approval doesn’t? These inconsistencies suggest hasty drafting without adequate consideration of regulatory architecture.

Relatedly, I’d also like to use my 1st Amendment rights to complain to my Constitution-ruled government. It seems like our rule of law is being dismantled systematically. (I could provide more examples than appear in the next paragraph, but it would mostly be tangential to this rule.) There appears to be a top-down cascade of lawlessness from things like the...
DOE characterizes this rule as limited to reactor testing, yet it modifies Part 851 provisions that apply across multiple DOE missions, creating impacts far beyond the Office of Nuclear Energy.
The proposal does not clearly define how the revised requirements will be confined to specific activities, increasing the likelihood of inconsistent interpretation across sites and programs.
By enabling reactor testing at new or nontraditional locations, the rule extends DOE safety oversight into environments that may lack mature safety systems, without evaluating readiness or risk.
DOE fails to analyze how these changes interact with existing safety obligations under Environmental Management, NNSA, or Office of Science operations.
Weakening safety requirements for one mission area creates a precedent that other DOE offices may invoke, accelerating regulatory erosion across the Department.
Newly introduced or revised terms lack sufficient clarity, increasing the risk of inconsistent application among contractors and DOE field offices.
Changes to definitions shift interpretive responsibility to DOE field elements, increasing variability and undermining uniform worker protection.
The rule qualifies as a significant regulatory action under EO 12866 due to its safety implications and policy novelty, yet DOE does not subject it to full OMB review.
DOE does not analyze less harmful alternatives, such as targeted exemptions or pilot programs, as required under EO 12866 decision making principles.
The proposal does not evaluate tradeoffs between accelerated timelines and increased safety risk, undermining informed decision-making.
DOE does not consider whether reduced preventive safety requirements will increase downstream costs related to injury response, claims, and corrective actions.
Removing structured safety program requirements reduces consistency and predictability in hazard identification and control.
The rule places excessive reliance on contractor judgment without establishing minimum enforceable benchmarks.
Generic OSHA-based approaches do not adequately address the unique risks of nuclear reactor testing environments.
The rule fails to assess how reduced structure disproportionately affects short-term, construction, and subcontracted workers.
Vague safety expectations limit workers’ ability to understand and assert their rights to a safe workplace.
Reduced regulatory clarity may discourage workers from reporting hazards due to uncertainty about enforceable standards.
Evaluating customized contractor safety programs without clear standards increases workload for DOE safety professionals.
DOE does not account for the time and cost required to retrain federal staff on revised requirements and definitions.
Greater discretion increases the likelihood of uneven enforcement outcomes across sites.
Reduced specificity makes it more difficult to identify noncompliance and assign corrective responsibility.
DOE does not evaluate how the proposed framework would have affected past safety incidents or enforcement challenges.
Weakening worker protections in nuclear environments risks undermining confidence in DOE’s commitment to safety.
This proposal therefore qualifies as a “significant regulatory action” under Executive Order 12866 and should have undergone full OMB/OIRA review.
DOE does not evaluate less harmful alternatives such as limited exemptions, pilot programs, or targeted streamlining despite EO 12866’s requirement to assess approaches that maximize net benefits.

Although framed as limited to nuclear reactor testing, the rule modifies Part 851 provisions that function as Department-wide worker safety requirements, with implications across multiple DOE programs, sites, and contractors.
DOE fails to analyze how changes to definitions, applicability, and safety expectations affect other DOE offices, including Environmental Management, NNSA, and the Office of Science.

By making previously mandatory safety requirements optional for one mission area, DOE establishes a precedent likely to drive similar deregulatory requests across the Department, without explaining how such erosion will be prevented.
Revised and newly introduced terminology lacks sufficient clarity, increasing the likelihood of inconsistent interpretation among contractors, field offices, and oversight personnel.

Although framed as a narrow reform to support reactor testing, this proposal alters core elements of DOE’s worker safety and health framework, with implications that extend well beyond a single office or mission. Changes to definitions, applicability, and safety expectations affect multiple DOE programs, contractors, locations, and workforces. DOE has not an...

Changes to definitions, applicability, and safety expectations will require updates to DOE orders, guidance documents, training materials, and oversight procedures well beyond the Office of Nuclear Energy.

Rather than reducing burden, the proposed changes are likely to increase workload for DOE safety professionals across multiple offices, who must now evaluate bespoke contractor safety programs without clear regulatory benchmarks.

The proposal does not clearly address how worker safety responsibilities will be divided among DOE offices, host institutions ( new reactor locations beyond DOE owned or lease properties), site operators, and contractors at newly authorized locations.

New testing locations are likely to rely on transient, construction, and subcontractor workforces who are particularly vulnerable to inconsistent or weakened safety requirements. Removing structured safety requirements exacerbates these risks by reducing clarity and accountability for worker protection at emerging sites.

The rule facilitates nuclear reactor testing at new locations, including non-traditional sites and facilities not previously subject to the same level of nuclear safety and worker protection oversight. DOE has not evaluated whether these locations possess the infrastructure or expertise necessary to safely operate under reduced regulatory requirements.
By failing to subject this proposal to full EO 12866 review, DOE deprives stakeholders of transparency regarding the true costs, risks, and tradeoffs of the rule.

Prior to the establishment of DOE-specific worker safety regulations, DOE facilities relied largely on general industry OSHA standards, a framework that proved insufficient for controlling the complex, high-hazard risks unique to DOE operations.

DOE appears to minimize the significance of the rule by characterizing changes as administrative or procedural, despite eliminating mandatory standards and structured safety requirements that directly affect working conditions.
The proposal raises novel policy issues by departing from DOE’s longstanding approach of requiring structured, consensus-based worker safety programs for nuclear operations.
The proposed rule fails to address how weakened requirements would have affected historical DOE safety failures, many of which occurred under less structured safety programs.
Vague safety expectations increase contractor legal exposure by making compliance determinations subjective and retrospective.

Standardized safety requirements reduce onboarding time, training costs, and error rates for workers moving between DOE sites. The proposed rule eliminates this efficiency. DOE is arguing against best practices used in aviation, nuclear power, and chemical manufacturing.
By allowing contractor-specific interpretations of ‘adequate protection,’ DOE creates inconsistent safety programs that require greater federal oversight, not less.
Ambiguity requires more documentation, not less—particularly during assessments, enforcement actions, and post-incident reviews.

DOE claims the rule will accelerate reactor testing timelines, yet fails to analyze the well-documented reality that injuries, near-misses, and safety stand-downs are among the most common causes of project delays. Reducing preventive safety requirements may increase the likelihood of work stoppages, investigations, and corrective actions directly underminin...
This rule reduces proven safety protections and could increase occupational injuries, exposures, and accidents.
Regulations should be updated to improve protection, not to remove established minimum requirements.
The changes weaken enforcement mechanisms, risking inconsistent safety outcomes across facilities.
Weakening worker safety standards erodes trust in DOE’s commitment to worker protection and fails to meet its statutory obligations.
This proposed rule undermines DOE’s own statutory mandate to protect workers by weakening regulatory requirements that were put in place after decades of learning from workplace injury and illness.
Using convenience or operational burden as justification to reduce safeguards undermines fundamental safety science and puts workers at unnecessary risk of cumulative harm.
Weakening enforcement and variance processes invites inconsistent implementation, undermining accountability and potentially elevating worker risk.
Equating OSHA minimums with protection needed in nuclear workplaces ignores the specialized nature and severity of nuclear hazards, potentially compromising worker health.

Eliminating a structured safety program (Appendix A: functional areas)removes essential consistency and oversight mechanisms, increasing risk variability and jeopardizing worker health in hazardous nuclear environments.

Removing mandatory consensus standards substantially weakens safety protections and invites inconsistent, contractor-dependent interpretations of risk, potentially increasing injuries, exposures, and long-term health consequences for workers. Consensus standards represent the industry’s best, empirically driven protections developed by safety professionals. ...

DOE’s failure to treat this rule as significant under EO 12866 is particularly problematic given that Congress expressly required DOE to regulate worker safety in high-hazard environments, making any weakening of such protections inherently significant.

DOE’s reliance on efficiency and burden reduction as justifications for weakening worker protections is inconsistent with Congress’s decision to impose DOE-specific safety regulations despite known costs. Congress accepted the cost of enhanced safety because the cost of worker injury, illness, and loss of life was deemed unacceptable.

Applying weakened safety requirements to new reactor testing locations contradicts Congress’s recognition that emerging and experimental environments require heightened,not reduced, worker protections.

Modifying variance and enforcement provisions to emphasize flexibility over accountability undermines Congress’s intent that DOE exercise strong federal oversight over contractor safety performance. Congress did not design DOE’s worker safety program to be negotiated at the project level.
Relegating Appendix A to optional guidance eliminates the very framework Congress expected DOE to use to ensure consistent, preventive worker protection across sites and contractors.
Making structured safety program elements optional directly conflicts with Congress’s requirement that DOE establish comprehensive, systematic approaches to hazard identification and control.

Allowing contractors increased discretion to define how they meet safety requirements contradicts Congress’s intent to impose mandatory, enforceable minimum protections due to past contractor failures.

DOE reframes the purpose of Part 851 to emphasize flexibility and efficiency rather than worker protection, contrary to Congress’s directive that worker safety be the primary objective of DOE-specific regulations.

Congress required DOE to promulgate and enforce its own worker safety and health regulations because general industry OSHA standards were not adequate to address the unique hazards present at DOE facilities, including nuclear, radiological, chemical, and high-hazard research operations.
The proposed rule reverses this statutory logic by treating OSHA minimums as presumptively sufficient in nuclear reactor testing environments.

Congress directed DOE to establish enforceable, preventive worker safety requirements precisely to avoid discretionary, contractor-defined safety practices that had historically failed to protect workers.
Nothing in DOE’s statutory authority permits the Department to weaken worker safety protections to accelerate mission execution or technology deployment.

This expanded applicability increases legal and administrative complexity without adequate justification. DOE fails to explain how the Department will ensure enforcement, oversight, and consistent application of safety requirements at non-DOE locations, particularly where state and local jurisdictions also have authority. Consensus standards represent best practices and scientific hazard thresholds; excluding them erodes protective safety baselines. OSHA standards are minimums, not protective upper bounds. Consensus standards reflect deeper industry and scientific consensus on hazard controls that go beyond OSHA floors. DOE’s selective exclusion disregards decades of occupati...

Changing Appendix A to Non-Mandatory Guidance Demoting Appendix A removes structural safety program expectations. Appendix A provides a functional framework to ensure consistency in hazard identification and control. Weakening variance processes degrades procedural safeguards. Existing variance and enforcement procedures support transparency, fairness, and consistency. The proposed changes concentrate decision-making in local offices without appropriate checks, increasing risk that standards will be diluted and enforcement actions inconsistent across workplaces.

The proposal does not evaluate how weakened worker safety requirements may increase the likelihood of claims under the Price-Anderson Act, workers’ compensation disputes, or tort litigation, thereby increasing financial and reputational risk to the Department.
